_ai: AI fills this. Derived from: non-functional-requirements.json + functional-requirements.json + business-rules.json. Do not edit manually.

architecture.hybridApproach: Synchronous saga orchestration for the booking write path (latency-critical, requires atomic compensation) combined with async choreography for cancellation and all downstream fan-out (notifications, analytics, dashboard updates). The read path uses CQRS with pre-aggregated materialized views.

corePatterns.usedFor: Appointment aggregate. Current state is derived by replaying the ordered AppointmentEvent log. Enables full audit trail, event replay for view rebuilds, and decoupled downstream consumers.

Guaranteed at-least-once event delivery. Events are written to an outbox table in the same DB transaction as the state change. The outbox-relay-service polls and publishes to SNS, eliminating dual-write risk.

Booking creation (UJ002). booking-command-service coordinates the three-step saga (reserve slot → capture payment → confirm appointment) and applies synchronous compensation on any failure. Ensures BR004 — no partial bookings.
Appointment cancellation (UJ003). Each step (slot release, refund, notification) reacts to the previous step's event independently. Slot release is never blocked by refund failure (BR009).

Dashboard and analytics read path. Writes go to the event store; reads are served from the pre-aggregated DashboardView materialized view. Enables p95 < 200ms dashboard reads independent of booking history depth.
All SQS message consumers. Implemented via event_id watermarks in an inbox deduplication table per service. Ensures at-least-once delivery from SQS never produces duplicate side effects.
booking-command-service deduplication of booking requests. booking_idempotency table with unique constraint on idempotency_key enforces BR006 — duplicate booking requests return the original result.
Notification Service. All notification content is derived from the event payload snapshot. notification-service never calls back into booking services, ensuring it is fully decoupled (BR007).
SNS topic per event type with SQS subscriber queues per consuming service. Adding a new consumer requires only a new SQS subscription — the producer is never modified (NFR015).

Multi-tenancy model. All tenants share a single PostgreSQL cluster. RLS policies on every table enforce tenant_id isolation at the database layer, safe-by-default (null context returns zero rows). Enforces BR005.

Failed events after retry exhaustion are routed to a per-service DLQ. Platform operators inspect, correct, and re-queue or discard via the ops-service console. Enforces NFR016 (4-hour resolution SLA).
DashboardView is a pre-aggregated table updated incrementally per booking event. Enables O(1) reads for the tenant dashboard independent of booking history volume.

services.name: api-gateway
services.responsibility: Single ingress point. Terminates TLS, validates JWTs, sets tenant_id context, generates correlation_id, and routes requests to downstream services. Enforces per-tenant rate limits.

services.name: tenant-service
services.responsibility: Tenant lifecycle management: registration, config, staff roster, service catalogue, suspension, deprovisioning. Authoritative source for all tenant metadata.

services.name: booking-command-service
services.responsibility: Booking saga orchestrator. Coordinates slot reservation, payment capture, and appointment confirmation atomically. Enforces idempotency and saga compensation.

services.name: availability-service
services.responsibility: Computes and serves open booking slots. Derives availability from working hours, confirmed appointments, and staff blocks. Enforces booking window and working hours constraints.

services.name: cancellation-saga-service
services.responsibility: Cancellation and rescheduling saga coordinator. Validates cancellation policy, releases slots, triggers refunds, and emits events for downstream choreography.

services.name: payment-service
services.responsibility: Anti-corruption layer over Stripe. Handles payment capture (synchronous, called by booking saga) and refund processing (async, via event subscription).

services.name: notification-service
services.responsibility: Async consumer of appointment lifecycle events. Dispatches email, SMS, and WhatsApp notifications. Fully decoupled — failure never propagates to booking operations.

services.name: dashboard-service
services.responsibility: Maintains the DashboardView materialized read model. Runs stream processing for anomaly detection (velocity spikes, cancellation rate). Serves tenant dashboard API from pre-aggregated view.

services.name: ops-service
services.responsibility: Internal platform operations for Platform Operators: DLQ inspection and re-queue, event replay, tenant data export, suspension, and deprovisioning.

services.name: outbox-relay-service
services.responsibility: Polls the PostgreSQL outbox table and publishes events to SNS. Guarantees at-least-once delivery without dual-write risk across all services.

adrs.title: Event Sourcing for Appointment aggregate
adrs.context: Appointment state must be auditable, replayable, and recoverable without data loss. Corrupt or stale read models must be rebuildable.
adrs.decision: Appointment state is stored as an ordered log of AppointmentEvent records. No mutable status column. Current state is computed by folding over the event log.
adrs.consequences: Full audit trail by construction. Dashboard and analytics views are rebuildable from event replay. Adds complexity: queries require event log fold or pre-aggregated read model.

adrs.title: PostgreSQL with Row-Level Security for multi-tenancy
adrs.context: BR005 requires zero cross-tenant data leakage under any condition including application bugs. Schema-per-tenant was ruled out due to operational complexity at scale.
adrs.decision: Single shared PostgreSQL cluster. RLS policies on every table enforce tenant_id isolation at the database layer. SET LOCAL app.tenant_id before every transaction. NULL context returns zero rows by default.
adrs.consequences: Tenant isolation enforced at two layers (application + DB). Eliminates an entire class of cross-tenant data leakage bugs. Adds RLS policy maintenance overhead. Enterprise tenants get a dedicated cluster.

adrs.title: Saga Orchestration for booking, Choreography for cancellation
adrs.context: Booking requires atomic success-or-failure (BR004) with synchronous compensation. Cancellation requires slot release to be independent of refund outcome (BR009).
adrs.decision: Booking uses Saga Orchestration: booking-command-service is the single coordinator, calls steps synchronously, compensates on failure. Cancellation uses Saga Choreography: each step reacts to the previous event independently.
adrs.consequences: Booking path achieves atomic compensation. Cancellation path is resilient to payment provider failures. Orchestration adds a central coordination point; choreography requires careful event ordering documentation.

adrs.title: Transactional Outbox for guaranteed event delivery
adrs.context: Without the Outbox pattern, a service that writes to the DB and then publishes to SNS risks losing the event if the service crashes between the two operations.
adrs.decision: Events are written to an outbox table in the same DB transaction as the state change. outbox-relay-service polls the table and publishes to SNS, then marks rows as published.
adrs.consequences: At-least-once delivery guaranteed. Events are never lost between DB write and SNS publish. Adds polling latency (target < 1 second). Consumers must be idempotent.

adrs.title: SNS + SQS for Pub/Sub fan-out
adrs.context: Multiple services need to react to booking events independently. Direct service-to-service calls would couple producers to consumers.
adrs.decision: SNS topic per event type. Each consumer subscribes with a dedicated SQS queue. New consumers added without modifying the producer.
adrs.consequences: Fully decoupled consumers (NFR015). SQS provides 14-day message retention for consumer recovery (NFR003). FIFO queues per tenant ensure per-tenant ordering. Adds AWS infrastructure complexity.

adrs.title: CQRS with Materialized View for dashboard
adrs.context: Dashboard must respond in < 200ms (NFR006). Computing metrics on demand from appointment history at scale would violate this target.
adrs.decision: Dashboard reads are served from a pre-aggregated DashboardView table, updated incrementally per booking event. Writes never touch the read model directly.
adrs.consequences: O(1) dashboard reads regardless of booking history depth. Dashboard lag < 5 seconds under normal load (NFR008). View is rebuildable from event replay on corruption.

adrs.title: SQS FIFO with MessageGroupId per tenant for noisy-neighbour isolation
adrs.context: A single high-volume tenant must not degrade event processing for other tenants (NFR011).
adrs.decision: SQS FIFO queues use tenant_id as MessageGroupId. Per-tenant throughput quotas enforced at API Gateway. Dedicated queues provisioned for Enterprise tenants.
adrs.consequences: High-volume tenant processing is isolated per-group. Per-tenant ordering guaranteed within a queue. Adds queue partitioning complexity for Enterprise tier.
